segments.s456-g17: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' modifying the speech. گفت is a simple past (3rd person) 'he said' with the subject omitted (typical in Persian). با is the preposition 'to/with' that introduces the addressees; موبدان is the plural of موبد (Zoroastrian priests) and is the first addressee; و links to ردان, another group being addressed. The phrase literally 'thus said to the mobeds and the ردان.'
segments.s456-g18: This is a nominal list in apposition to the addressees: بزرگان 'the great ones/elders' and بیداردل 'the wakeful‑hearted' qualify بخردان. بخردان (بِ+خردان) here names the wise/prudent persons being addressed — an epithet applied to those elders. Word order places the modifiers before the head noun, a frequent poetic pattern.
segments.s456-g19: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). گیتی is the subject 'the world'. نجستم is past first‑person with the negative prefix ن‑ + جستم, here meaning 'I did not obtain / I did not win/seek' (classical usage is compact and can mean 'I did not come by' or 'I did not gain'). به رنج و به داد uses the preposition به in an instrumental sense 'by/through' and lists the means 'by toil and by justice'.
segments.s456-g20: مرا is the object/clitic form 'me' (here marking the person affected). تاج یزدان is a noun phrase 'the crown of God' (تاج 'crown', یزدان 'God'). به سر is a locative phrase 'upon (the) head'. بر نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/put on' with بر indicating 'on/upon' — together 'placed upon (my) head.'
segments.s456-g21: شگفتی‌تر is the comparative 'more astonishing' (تر comparative suffix). از کار من introduces the standard of comparison 'than my deed' (از marks the thing compared to). در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world' specifying the domain of comparison. Together this fragment sets up 'more astonishing than my deed in the world...'.
segments.s456-g22: نبیند is a negative present/stative form meaning 'sees not' (3rd person), used impersonally here with کسی ('no one') to yield 'no one sees'. آشکار و نهان are paired adjectives/adverbs 'openly and hiddenly' (i.e. in either visible or secret matters) that qualify the scope of 'sees'. The order 'نبیند کسی' is a typical classical Persian inversion for 'no one sees'.
segments.s456-g23: ندانیم is the 1st‑person plural present with negative prefix ن‑: 'we do not/know not'. جز 'except' restricts the statement. داد پاداش این is a noun phrase 'this reward of justice' (داد 'justice' modifying پاداش 'reward', and این 'this' pointing to it). The whole fragment says 'we know nothing except this just reward.'
segments.s456-g24: که introduces the content clause 'that...'. بر ما here means 'about/for us' (topics or the object of the forthcoming action). پس از ما is a temporal phrase 'after us' (پس 'after' + از + ما). کنند is 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/present 'they do / they will do' used to report what others will do. آفرین functions as an acclamation/noun 'praise' or the exclamation 'afarin' — the sense here is that 'they will say/offer praise' about us after we are gone.
segments.s456-g49: به هر سو is a directional prepositional phrase ('towards every side'); فرستاد is past simple 3rd‑person singular 'sent/dispatched' and governs the object سپاه. بی‌مر is an archaic/poetic modifier placed before سپاه (in poetry modifiers can precede the noun) and qualifies سپاه as unstinting/unwearied or otherwise emphatic. The overall order (direction → verb → modified object) is typical of Ferdowsi's flexible poetic syntax.
segments.s456-g50: ز here functions as 'because of/with regard to' (not the simple 'from'); همی داشت is a periphrastic continuous construction ('kept/was keeping') with داشت as the auxiliary-like verb. گیتی is the noun 'the world' and نگاه is the object/thing kept (i.e. 'a watch, gaze'); the clause therefore reads 'because of the enemy the world was keeping its gaze/watch.'
segments.s456-g51: کرد is past simple 'made/caused'; بی‌بیم is a predicative adjective 'free from fear / fearless'. The phrase is elliptical/poetic: one can expand it to جهان را از بداندیش بی‌بیم کرد ('He made the world free of ill‑wishers'), i.e. the direct object marker را is omitted as is common in poetry and the prepositional از indicates 'free of / from (the) ill‑minded'.
segments.s456-g52: دل بدسگالان is a genitive phrase 'the hearts of the ill‑omened' (دل = 'hearts'); بدو is the dative 'to him/unto him' showing the direction of inclination, نیم is 'half/partly' functioning adverbially ('partly/halfway'), and کرد again is 'made/caused'. Together the clause means 'he caused the hearts of the ill‑omened to incline toward him in part' — i.e. he partly won their hearts.
segments.s456-g41: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بگشاد is a past action (ب‑ prefix plus root گشاد/گشودن) meaning 'opened' or 'relieved' and appears before its subject in this classical order. داننده is an agent noun 'the one who knows / the expert' (the actor); از + آب‌بند marks the source/location 'out of/from the dam'.
segments.s456-g42: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an item). شهر is the direct object 'a city'. فرمود here functions as an act-of-creation/ordering ('caused/established' — classical Persian often uses فرمود with a causative/performative sense), with بس + سودمند as a following predicative phrase: بس is an intensifier/limiter (here 'very' / 'sufficiently') and سودمند is the adjective 'useful'. Word order: subject/actor is implicit in the narrative; the verb frames 'made/established a certain very-useful city.'
segments.s456-g43: چو = 'when'. دیوار شهر is the noun phrase 'the city's wall'. اندرآورد is a past form 'brought in/erected' (اندر + آورد), and گرد here functions adverbially as 'around' — the clause says he set/raised the wall around the city. The verb precedes or accompanies its object in the classical narrative order.
segments.s456-g44: ورا is the unstressed object pronoun/adverb 'to it / for it'. نام + کردند is a light-verb construction: the noun نام ('name') plus کردند ('they made/did') = 'they named (it)'. داراب گرد is the name given; گرد here is part of the place-name element (‑gerd/‑gird) meaning a settled/round place — together 'Darab‑gerd'.
segments.s456-g45: یکی introduces an indefinite subject 'a certain one'. آتش افروخت is a past action 'kindled a fire' (آفروخت = lit/kindled). از + تیغ کوه is a spatial/source phrase: از 'from' or 'on' with تیغ کوه ('the mountain's ridge/point'); poetically it indicates the fire was set on or from the mountain's crest.
segments.s456-g46: پرستنده آذر is a genitive/attributive phrase 'worshipper(s) of Fire' (classical Persian often omits the ezāfe). آمد is the past tense 'came'; گروه is postposed and specifies that it was a 'group/troop' — together: 'a troop of fire‑worshippers came.'
segments.s456-g47: ز (poetic form of از) means 'from'. هر پیشه‌ای = 'every/each craft' (پیشه with the indefinite suffix). کارگر is the object 'workers'. خواستند is the past plural 'they requested/they wanted' — so the clause means 'they sought workers from every trade.'
segments.s456-g48: همی is a narrative particle used here to mark continuous or emphatic action ('then / so / they proceeded to'). شهر ایران is the noun phrase 'the city of Iran' (possessive). بیاراستند is the past plural verb 'they adorned / they beautified' (بی‑آراستن with past ending). The clause: 'so they adorned the Iranian city.'
segments.s456-g1: کنون is an adverb 'now' placed frontally to set the time. آفرین here is a noun meaning 'praise/acclamation' (not the verb 'to create'); it forms a noun phrase with جهان‌آفرین, a compound 'world‑creator' used as the thing being praised.
segments.s456-g2: بخوانیم is the present-subjunctive/optative first‑person plural 'let us recite/let us sing' (we should recite). بر introduces the beneficiary/target ('for/upon') and شهریار زمین is the beneficiary NP 'the sovereign/king of the earth' (شهریار = king, زمین = earth). The object of بخوانیم is the preceding noun phrase 'آفرین جهان‌آفرین'.
segments.s456-g3: ابوالقاسم is a proper name (the poet's kunya). آن is the demonstrative 'that' (here functioning like a definite article/pointing word), and شاه خورشید چهر is an appositive NP 'the king whose face is like the sun' (خورشید + چهر = 'sun‑faced'). The whole phrase reads as name + descriptive apposition.
segments.s456-g4: بیاراست is an archaic perfect/past-form meaning 'adorned' (he/she adorned). گیتی is the direct object 'the world'. به here is instrumental/causal 'by/with' and is repeated before each noun to mark the means: به داد و به مهر = 'by justice and by kindness'. داد = 'justice', مهر = 'kindness/affection'.
segments.s456-g5: نجوید here is the present form of جوید with the negative prefix ن, so 'does not seek' (classical phrasing often reads as 'seeks nothing but...'). جز از = 'except/other than' introduces the exclusive complement 'خوبی و راستی' = 'goodness and truth/uprightness.'
segments.s456-g6: نیارد is an archaic negative form likely corresponding to نیاورد ('does not bring/produce'), i.e. 'he does not cause/bring'. بداد is ambiguous here in form and meaning (its provided sense list does not supply the sense used in the line), but grammatically it is part of the verb phrase; اندرون کاستی is the object phrase 'deficiency/lack into the inner (self)'. Word order is verb + object. (Because the supplied lemma/senses for نیارد and بداد do not match the needed verbal senses, their senses are marked below as unavailable.)
segments.s456-g7: This clause is an optative wish finished by باد (here 'may'). جهان روشن is a nominal clause 'the world [be] bright/illuminated.' از تاج محمود gives the source/agent 'from/by the crown of Mahmud' (از used causally/locatively), and باد is the optative particle 'may'.
segments.s456-g8: همه روزگارانش = 'all his days/ages' (روزگاران with the 3rd‑person possessive suffix -ش 'his'). مسعود is an adjective/name meaning 'fortunate' here, and باد again is the optative 'may' giving the wish 'may all his days be fortunate.'
segments.s456-g9: همیشه (adverb) precedes the adjective جوان to give a temporal/continuous sense: 'always young'. تا introduces the limiting period 'until/while' and takes the noun جوانی as its bound; بود is the past copula 'was/ remained'. The clause thus states a continuous past condition limited by 'youth'.
segments.s456-g10: همان here functions as 'likewise/just so' linking this clause to the previous; زنده is a predicative adjective 'alive'. تا + زندگانی again marks the temporal limit 'until life (lasted)'; بود is the past copula. The pattern mirrors the first clause (parallelism common in poetry).
segments.s456-g11: چه گفت is an inverted interrogative: 'what (did he) say'; in older/poetic Persian the verb follows چه directly. آن qualifies the following noun phrase. سراینده (lit. 'one who composes/sings') is the head noun, modified by دهقان پیر 'old peasant'—two appositive modifiers describing the speaker.
segments.s456-g12: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/about' introducing the topics of the poet's utterance: 'from/about Gushtasp'. The following وز is a poetic contraction of و ز (i.e. 'and from'), linking a second source. نامدار is an adjective 'renowned' modifying اردشیر; the whole phrase lists the two famous names the poet spoke of.
segments.s456-g13: وزان is a poetic compound (و + ز + آن) meaning 'and from those' or 'from that group'; it introduces a list. نامداران is plural 'renowned ones' and پاکیزه‌رای is an adjectival epithet 'pure-minded/clean of counsel' modifying them. The phrase groups a set of distinguished people.
segments.s456-g14: ز introduces another item 'from Darab'. The following وز again functions as the poetic 'and from' joining the next item. رسم و رای is a coordinated noun phrase 'custom and counsel' (رای here 'counsel/opinion'), with همای ('Homay') as the possessor/source (i.e. 'the customs and counsel of Homay').
segments.s456-g15: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal subordinate clause. دارا is the subject (here a proper name); به تخت مهی is a prepositional phrase 'upon the moon‑bright throne' (مهی = 'moon‑like/beautiful'). برنشست is the simple past 'sat upon' (verb at the clause end, as typical).
segments.s456-g16: This coordinate clause gives two successive actions of the same subject: کمر بر میان بست is an idiomatic transitive structure 'he girded his loins (bound a belt) about his middle'—کمر here = 'girdle/belt' and بست = 'bound/tied'. و introduces the second verb بگشاد دست 'he opened/spread his hands' (بگشاد used here as a past verb form meaning 'opened/expanded').
segments.s456-g25: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal construction: نباید (should not / it must not be) + که introducing the subordinate clause. پیچد is a 3rd-person singular classical-form verb placed before its subject کس (literary V–S order); here it carries the idea of 'twist/turn' in a metaphorical sense (i.e., to wring oneself with pleasure or to gloat). از رنج ما is a prepositional phrase (از = 'from/because of', رنج = 'suffering') marking the cause or object of that (disapproved) attitude; ما is the possessive/agent 'our'.
segments.s456-g26: This is a continuation of the cause/objects introduced by از: ز (poetic variant of از) links the following nouns to the previous clause. بیشی means 'excess, surplus' (here 'excess' or 'overabundance'), و is the coordinating 'and', آگندن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'soiling/defiling' (to soil), and گنج ما is the noun + postposed possessor 'our treasure'. The whole phrase lists further things one should not gloat over.
segments.s456-g27: زمانه is the subject 'the age / the world'. ز (poetic 'from/through') + داد (here the noun 'justice') + من (the possessor 'my' expressed postpositively, a common poetic order: noun + possessor) yields 'through my justice'. آباد is an adjective/predicate 'prosperous', and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical wishes use باد after the predicate).
segments.s456-g28: دل is the subject 'heart(s)'; زیر دستان ما is a locative/relational phrase 'under our hands' where زیر = 'under / subject to' and دستان (hands) takes the possessor ما after it (poetic order: noun + possessor). شاد is the adjective 'happy/joyful', and باد again is the optative 'may'. The whole clause is a parallel wish to the previous: 'May the hearts under our rule be glad.'
segments.s456-g29: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereafter' (ازان = 'from that', پس = 'after'). The repeated ز (poetic از) before هندوستان and روم marks origin ('from India and from Rome'); the repeated preposition is a rhetorical/poetic device for balance.
segments.s456-g30: ز هر مرز is 'from every border/region' (ز = from, هر = every, مرز = frontier/limit). باارز is an adjective 'esteemed/valuable' (archaic/compound form), و = 'and', آباد = 'prosperous', and بوم is the noun 'homeland / land' serving as the head of this nominal description; the verb (came/appeared) is omitted or implied in poetic narrative, so this functions as a nominative phrase describing what came from those regions.
segments.s456-g31: برفتند is the simple past 3rd-person-plural 'they went / they departed' (literary prefix بر- + رفتند). با هدیه و با نثار is a coordinated instrumental phrase 'with gift(s) and with offering(s)' (با = 'with', هدیه = 'gift/tribute', نثار = 'offering/tribute'), showing what they took or carried as they left.
segments.s456-g32: بجستند is a past-tense verb 'they sought' (classical poetic form where the verb precedes the object). خشنودی شهریار is a genitival noun phrase: خشنودی 'pleasure/approval' with شهریار 'the king' following as its possessor, so 'the king's pleasure/favour' — together meaning 'they sought the king's approval/favour.'
segments.s456-g33: چنان بد که is a fixed poetic concessive/degree phrase: 'so/so badly that...'. روزی is a temporal adverb 'one day'. ز بهر (poetic compound) = 'for the sake of / on account of' and introduces the purpose 'for the flock'. Word order is typical older Persian: degree phrase + time + prepositional purpose.
segments.s456-g34: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. The following که introduces a purpose/result clause and here functions like 'so that / in order that' (hence the subjunctive). اسپان is the object 'the horses'. ببیند is a present-subjunctive/optative 3rd‑singular used after that/که to express purpose ('that he might see' / 'to see'). یله is a predicative adjective 'loose, unrestrained', describing اسپان.
segments.s456-g35: ز marks source 'from' (poetic). پستی here denotes the low place/low ground. برآمد is simple past 'he rose/ascended'. به + noun (کوهی) marks the goal/direction 'to a mountain', and رسید is 'he reached'. This is a sequence of past actions with simple verbs.
segments.s456-g36: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain' introducing the following NP. The adjectives بی‌کران (boundless) and ژرف (deep) precede the head noun دریا (sea), forming 'a certain boundless, deep sea'. بدید is past 'he saw' (simple narrative past).
segments.s456-g37: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered' (often used for royal speech). کز here introduces the content/source clause and functions as 'that / from' (classical usage). روم and هندوان are sources ('from Rome' and 'from the Indians'); و is the conjunction. Note: وز appears as a poetic variant here; its meaning is 'from' (classical/poetic 'وز' ~ 'از'), though the listed modern senses don't reflect that.
segments.s456-g38: بیارند is the present-subjunctive/plural of آوردن 'to bring' used in reported orders ('let them bring' / 'they should bring'). کارآزموده is an adjective 'experienced, proven in work'. گوان is an archaic/poetic postpositive element that here helps pluralize/characterize those brought—'those who are such' or 'such ones'. Together they mean 'bring experienced men/people'.
segments.s456-g39: بجویند is present-subjunctive/plural of جویدن/جست‌و‌جوکردن 'let them seek / they should seek' and continues the commandive sequence. زان = 'from that' (demonstrative + preposition). آب دریا دری is a compact noun phrase: آب 'water' qualified by دریا 'sea' and دری as an adjectival/attributive 'marine / of the sea' — 'water of that sea' (sea-water).
segments.s456-g40: رسانند is present-subjunctive/plural of رساندن 'let them deliver / they should convey', forming the consequent purpose: deliver/bring. رودی is an indefinite object 'a river' (رود + -ی). به marks the recipient/goal 'to', هر کشوری = 'every country' (هر 'each/every' + کشوری 'country/realm'). Word order places the object (رودی) before the verb, acceptable in classical Persian.

sentences.s456-s9: Thus he said to the mobeds and the redān, to the great men and to the wakeful‑hearted wise.
sentences.s456-s10: For I did not obtain the world by toil nor by justice; the crown of God He placed upon my head.
sentences.s456-s11: None in the world sees a deed more astonishing than mine, whether open or hidden.
sentences.s456-s12: We know nothing but this reward of justice: that after us they will utter praise of us.
sentences.s456-s25: He dispatched troops to every quarter; because of the enemy the world kept watch.
sentences.s456-s26: He made the world free of ill‑wishers, and he partially won the hearts of the ill‑omened for himself.
sentences.s456-s21: When the expert opened the dam, he established a certain city, very useful.
sentences.s456-s22: When he brought the city's wall around it, they named it Darab-gerd.
sentences.s456-s23: A certain man kindled a fire on the mountain's ridge, and a band of fire-worshippers arrived.
sentences.s456-s24: They called for workers of every craft and thus adorned the city of Iran.
sentences.s456-s1: Now let us sing the praise of the Creator of the world for the sovereign of the earth.
sentences.s456-s2: Abu'l‑Qāsim, that sun‑faced king, adorned the world with justice and with kindness.
sentences.s456-s3: He seeks nothing but goodness and uprightness; his giving brings no lack into the inner self.
sentences.s456-s4: May the world be bright through Mahmud's crown; may all his days be fortunate.
sentences.s456-s5: He remained always young while he had youth, and likewise lived as long as life lasted.
sentences.s456-s6: What did that old peasant-poet say about Gushtasp and the famed Ardashir?
sentences.s456-s7: And from those renowned, the pure‑minded ones: from Darab, and from the customs and counsel of Homay.
sentences.s456-s8: When Dāra sat upon the moon‑bright throne, he girded his loins and spread his hands.
sentences.s456-s13: No one should gloat over our suffering, nor over the excess and the soiling of our treasure.
sentences.s456-s14: May the age be prosperous through my justice; may the hearts under our hands be glad.
sentences.s456-s15: After that, from India and from Rome, from every frontier came valued and prosperous homelands.
sentences.s456-s16: They departed with gifts and with offerings; they sought the king's favour.
sentences.s456-s17: It so happened that one day, to tend the flock, he came to see the horses loose.
sentences.s456-s18: He rose up from the low ground, reached a mountain, and beheld a boundless, deep sea.
sentences.s456-s19: He commanded that they bring (it) from Rome and from the Indians, experienced (men) such as those.
sentences.s456-s20: Have them fetch water from that sea and convey a river to every country.
